questions.prompt: A stock will pay $4 in dividends next year, growing 4% forever. Cost of equity is 10%. Using the Gordon DDM, what is the fair value per share? Answer in dollars.
Why does the Gordon DDM say a non-dividend-paying tech company is worth $0?
choices: Because the company actually is worthless
Because Gordon discounts dividends only — if D = 0 forever, the sum is 0. The result is wrong; the company isn't worthless, the model is the wrong tool
Because the cost of equity is too high
Because growth exceeds the discount rate
questions.explanation: Gordon's special-case assumption (constant D, growing at g) breaks when there are no dividends. Use FCFE — discounts the cash the firm *could* pay out, not what it does pay.

questions.prompt: When should you prefer FCFE over DDM for valuing equity?
choices: Firm pays little or no dividend
Firm has stable, predictable dividend policy
Firm uses buybacks instead of dividends
You want results identical to DDM under any payout policy
questions.explanation: FCFE handles non-payers and buyback users correctly. DDM is fine for stable dividend policy; under 100% payout they're identical.

questions.prompt: A multi-stage DDM: D_1 = $2, growing 12% per year for 5 years (so D_5 = $3.15), then 3% forever. r_E = 10%. What is the terminal value at end of year 5 (the Gordon TV at that date)? Answer in $.

furtherReading.title: The Power of Expectations: Nvidia's Earnings and the Market Reaction
furtherReading.why: Damodaran's own DCF on NVIDIA ($87 intrinsic vs $106 market) is a concrete, named practitioner's valuation of the central AI stock — not a textbook explainer.
